mappings.rationale: Directly covers the nature and scope of the Earth sciences including geology, geophysics, and Earth systems discussed throughout this section.
Section B covers the nature, scope, and methods of the individual Earth sciences, with geology as the foundational discipline.
Covers the integrated study of Earth's systems, relevant to sections B.1 (physical geography) and B.6 (atmospheric sciences) and their interconnections.
Section A.5.a.vi specifically covers the theory of plate tectonics as a unification of continental drift and seafloor spreading.
Section A.2.a.ii covers speculations about fossils, A.4.a.ii covers development of paleontology and stratigraphy, and A.5.a.v covers advances in paleontology.
Section A.4.a.i covers classification of rocks, A.5.a.ii covers experimental petrology, and rocks are fundamental to geologic sciences.
Section A.3.a.i covers the beginnings of mineralogy including the study of ore deposits, and A.4.a.i covers classification of minerals.
Section A.2.a.i covers speculations about volcanic eruptions, and volcanology is part of the geologic sciences discussed in section B.
Section A.4.a.iv specifically covers evidence for an Ice Age and the work of Louis Agassiz.
Section A.5.b.v covers the study of glacier motion and high-latitude ice sheets, directly relevant to glaciation.
Section A.5.c covers atmospheric sciences including the classification of climate and weather modification, and climate change is central to modern Earth sciences.
Section A.5.c.v covers the classification of climate, and section B.6 covers atmospheric sciences as an Earth science discipline.
Section A.4.c covers the study of weather and climate, and A.5.c.ii covers advances in weather forecasting.
Section A.3.c covers atmospheric sciences from early studies through modern technology applications, and B.6 covers atmospheric sciences as a discipline.
Section A.4.b.ii covers the beginnings of oceanography, and A.5.b covers study of ocean circulation, currents, and ocean floor charting.

mappings.vsiAuthor: David George Bowers, Emyr Martyn Roberts
mappings.rationale: Section A.2.b.iii covers the study of tides, and tidal phenomena are part of hydrologic sciences discussed in section B.
Section A.4.b.i covers the study of groundwater flow and surface water discharge, and rivers are central to hydrologic sciences.
Lakes are part of the hydrologic sciences covered in sections A.4.b and A.5.b, including water resources and groundwater studies.
Deserts represent a geomorphological environment covered under physical geography (B.1) and landform studies.
Mountains are key landforms covered under physical geography (B.1) and relate to plate tectonics and geomorphology.
Section A.4.a.vi covers concepts of landform evolution, and B.1 covers physical geography including landforms.
Coral reefs relate to marine geology, oceanography, and paleontology covered in sections A.5.b and B.

mappings.vsiAuthor: John A. Matthews, David T. Herbert
mappings.rationale: Section B.1 covers physical geography including the distribution and spatial patterns of soils, water, climate, and landforms.
The Antarctic is studied through multiple Earth science disciplines including glaciology (A.5.b.v), atmospheric sciences, and ocean sciences.
The Arctic is studied through multiple Earth science disciplines including glaciology (A.5.b.v), atmospheric sciences, and ocean sciences.
Section A.5.a.iii covers advances in geophysics including seismology and the study of Earth's internal structure.
Covers earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and climate hazards that are studied through the Earth sciences described in sections A and B.
Savannas represent a biogeographic and climatic zone studied through physical geography (B.1) and climate classification.
Biogeography connects to physical geography (B.1) and the study of spatial patterns of soil, water, climate, and landforms.
Section A.5.a.i covers the development of radiometric dating, which relies on radioactivity to determine geological time.
Sections A.4.a.ii and A.5.a.v cover paleontological advances including faunal succession and Precambrian life, which document the history of life on Earth.
Dinosaurs are a major subject of paleontology, covered in the advances in paleontology discussed in sections A.4.a.ii and A.5.a.v.
Mass extinctions are studied through paleontology and stratigraphy covered in sections A.4.a.ii and A.5.a.v.
The Anthropocene represents a proposed geological epoch, directly connecting to the geologic and atmospheric sciences covered in this section.
Navigation historically depended on understanding of Earth sciences including geography, atmospheric sciences, and ocean currents.
